title: Acculturation and fertility transitions: evidence from China's rural-urban migrants
abstract: The migration-fertility relationship plays a pivotal role in shaping population dynamics and demographic structures. While previous studies predominantly emphasize economic assimilation's effect on migrants' fertility behaviors, the impact of acculturation remain empirically underexplored. Employing a multidimensional and bidirectional framework of acculturation and survey data from Guangzhou's rural-urban migrants, this study investigates how both urban (destination) and rural (origin) cultures jointly influence migrants' fertility behaviors, assessed by family size (number of children) and son preference (sex-selective practices for second births). Through Latent Class Analysis, we identified four distinct acculturation types: two integration subtypes (urban-dominant and rural-dominant) and two separation subtypes (stable and integration-tending). Results demonstrate significant acculturative effects: integration-group migrants exhibit both (1) a lower likelihood of having two or more children and (2) a reduced propensity for male-biased second births relative to separation groups. By analyzing the complex interplay of urban-rural cultural forces, this study substantially advances our understanding of acculturation-driven fertility transitions and yields policy implications for China's demographic challenges.
authors_text: Yue, Zhongshan; Gao, Liyang; Wen, Ming; Li, Shuzhuo
